content:
They quit to free the database and built a home no single vendor could rule. The owner replied in twenty-nine days, and the fine print said the opposite.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Linus Nylund on UnsplashA group of engineers who carry more production database scars than the summation of most company employees stood up an independent, vendor-neutral foundation on May 27, 2026, in Raleigh, North Carolina.
The launch announcement made it clear and intentional: The foundation is “not connected to or supported by Oracle.”
They were walking away from one company’s control over a single piece of software. This was not a hobby project or a research toy.
It runs inside Facebook, YouTube, Netflix, Uber, Shopify, and Booking.com, and under millions of WordPress sites. Your bank, your airline, and the app you opened this morning almost certainly touch it every time you tap the screen.
It is MySQL.
According to the DB-Engines popularity ranking, it is the second most popular database in the world and the most popular open source one, sitting just behind Oracle’s flagship database[1]. Oracle owns them both.
